symptom: VM deployment fails when using custom user image created in Azure Stack Hub version 1907
rootCause: Bug in Azure Stack Hub 1907 image handling; orchestration issue between IBHU and non-HA VM repair during image-based deployments
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.12.44 (KB 4517473, released Aug 16 2019). Fixes orchestration between IBHU and non-HA VM repair and updates URP to use ScopedRepair action plan.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.12.44

symptom: Internal secret rotation fails on Azure Stack Hub environments originally deployed on version 1901 or earlier
rootCause: Bug in internal secret rotation process introduced in 1907 that does not account for older deployment configurations from 1901 and earlier
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.15.50 (KB 4520315, released Sep 5 2019). Suppresses NC cluster certificate warning in test-azurestack to unblock PNU and internal secret rotation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.15.50

symptom: Azure Stack Hub operators cannot perform update or secret rotation because test-azurestack updatereadiness blocks admin operations when a pending secret/certificate expiration is detected
rootCause: test-azurestack updatereadiness incorrectly surfaced NC cluster certificate warnings as blocking conditions, preventing PNU and secret rotation operations
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.15.50 (KB 4520315). NC cluster certificate warning suppressed in test-azurestack updatereadiness so it no longer blocks admin operations.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.15.50

symptom: OEM Firmware updates required for Azure Stack Hub 1908 upgrade fail to install on 1907; false BMC saturation alerts generate high alert volume in 1907
rootCause: OEM firmware update installation had reliability issues (FRU timeout too short for Scale-Outs exceeding 6 hours); BMC runner generating false positive saturation alerts
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.17.54 (KB 4523826, released Sep 27 2019). Increases FRU timeout for Scale-Outs, fixes OEM update preparation reliability, and disables false-positive BMC saturation alerts.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.17.54

symptom: Deleting a load balancer in Azure Stack Hub 1907 results in orphaned resources; LB object persists in Network Controller (NC) after deletion
rootCause: Load balancer deletion did not propagate to delete the corresponding object in Network Controller (NC), leaving orphaned NC resources
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.17.54 (KB 4523826). Fix ensures deleting an LB also deletes the object in NC, preventing orphaned resources.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.17.54

symptom: DiskRP does not update application manifest parameters after completing Internal Secret Rotation in Azure Stack Hub 1907; Service Fabric ImageStoreService replicas are down
rootCause: DiskRP service did not refresh application manifest parameters following Internal Secret Rotation; unused SF DNS service could crash XRP during future updates; SF ImageStoreService replica failures
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.18.56 (KB 4528552, released Oct 29 2019). Fixes DiskRP manifest parameter update after secret rotation; disables unused SF DNS service; backports repair of SF ImageStoreService replicas.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.18.56

symptom: Non-HA VMs (NonHAVMs) fail to start after an Azure Stack Hub update in version 1907; VMs remain stopped post-update
rootCause: NonHAVMs initialization issue with guardian certificates; RestoreNonHAVMs process did not handle guardian cert initialization correctly during Azure Stack startup after update
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.26.70 (KB 4541348, released Feb 25 2020). Fixes NonHAVMs guardian certificate initialization; fixes RestoreNonHAVMs initialization issue during Azure Stack update.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.26.70

symptom: External certificate expiration alerts continue appearing on Azure Stack Hub admin portal even after successfully completing external secret rotation
rootCause: Certificate alerting was not scoped to only certificates bound to external endpoints; alerts fired for internal certificates that do not require external rotation
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.29.80 (KB 4555650, released Apr 2 2020). Certificate alerting fixed to only alert on certificates bound to external endpoints.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.29.80

symptom: Azure Stack Hub marketplace downloads fail with a certificate validation error
rootCause: Azure Bridge certificates were missing on WAS (Windows Azure Stack) and XRP VMs, causing certificate validation failure during marketplace download operations
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.30.83 (KB 4562962, released Nov 6 2020). Installs Azure Bridge Certificates on WAS and XRP VMs to resolve marketplace certificate validation errors.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.30.83

symptom: Test-AzureStack reports false Fabric Ring health failures (misdiagnosis) in Azure Stack Hub 1907; OEM firmware update fails due to alternate signer check blocking valid update scripts
rootCause: Test-AzureStack incorrectly diagnosed Fabric Ring health status; alternate signer checking was too restrictive for OEM firmware update scripts
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.7.35 (KB 4515310, released Jul 25 2019). Corrects Test-AzureStack Fabric Ring health diagnosis; skips alternate signer checking for OEM firmware update scripts.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.7.35

symptom: Azure Stack Hub environments originally deployed before version 1809 are missing OEM-related categories in CloudParameters, causing OEM update compatibility failures in 1907
rootCause: Pre-1809 deployments were not provisioned with certain OEM-related CloudParameters categories required for OEM compatibility in later versions
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.8.37 (KB 4515650, released Aug 2 2019). Adds missing OEM parameter categories to pre-1809 deployments via BeforeAzureStack inline patch.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.8.37

symptom: Azure Stack Hub admin portal does not display download progress for update packages; download progress bar missing or stalled
rootCause: URP (Update Resource Provider) was not reporting download progress to the admin portal UI; missing telemetry/UI update path for download progress in 1908
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1908.11.47 (KB 4535000, released Dec 18 2019). Improves admin portal to show download progress of Azure Stack Hub update packages.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1908/1.1908.11.47

symptom: Setting VM LicenseType to value None in Azure Stack Hub 1908 fails; None is not recognized as a valid LicenseType value
rootCause: CRP (Compute Resource Provider) did not handle LicenseType value None as valid; memory cache issue with virtual identifier lookup by network identity token
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1908.11.47 (KB 4535000). CRP updated to accept LicenseType None; memory cache for virtual identifier lookup by network identity token fixed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1908/1.1908.11.47

symptom: Azure Stack Hub 1907 update preparation steps are unreliable or stall; update preparation UX shows incorrect status
rootCause: WaitAndReceiveJob was too permissive of PowerShell states; UX reporting gaps in preparation phase; multiple preparation step reliability issues
solution: Apply hotfix 1.1907.18.56 (KB 4528552). Multiple preparation UX improvements and reliability fixes including stricter PowerShell state handling in WaitAndReceiveJob.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAdaptiveCloud/AzureAdaptiveCloud.wiki:/Azure Stack Hub/Builds/1907/1.1907.18.56

symptom: Unable to download helm charts in Azure Arc.
rootCause: The issue is with spec.
solution: Check the YAML file where you have mentioned to download the helm charts from helm repository the spec should be repository in the file.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#150494

symptom: Fluentd monitoring container keeps getting crashed
rootCause: Fluentd container gets crash with out of memory error because the version 1.2.1 has limit of 100Mi.
solution: The next version 1.3.0 has a fix where the limits of memory increased from 100Mi to 150Mi for the fluentd.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#167634

symptom: Azure Arc Resource Bridge installation fails with KVAError or PrepareKvaTimeoutError when using 32-bit MSI version of Azure CLI.
rootCause: Resource Bridge deployment requires 64-bit Azure CLI. The installation failed when attempted with the 32-bit MSI version.
solution: Uninstall the 32-bit Azure CLI, download and install Python 3.8.8 64-bit, then install Azure CLI via pip. Verify with: az --version | Select-String 64bit.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#169439

symptom: During Azure Arc resource bridge onboarding for VMware, Appliance creation fails with error Appliance Deploy timed out. Occurs when credentials contain backslash character.
rootCause: Known bug for vSphere where backslash in credentials causes interpretation error in one of the pods running on control plane VM.
solution: As a workaround, create an account without backslash character and use that to deploy the appliance. PG team is working on a fix for future release.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#169440

symptom: Customer was facing issue while deploying Google Kubernetes Engine cluster on Azure ARC. Customer was using GKE auto-pilot cluster. Azure ARC onboarding was failing with FailedScheduling - Insufficient cpu, Insufficient memory.
rootCause: Google Kubernetes Engine cluster with Auto-Pilot mode is not supported yet. The failure was due to insufficient resources on the Kubernetes cluster.
solution: Issue was fixed after deploying Google Kubernetes Engine k8s cluster in standard mode.

symptom: This article discusses Azure Arc ESU solution when devices are not connected to internet.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#183088

symptom: Year 4 Extended Security Updates fail to install on Azure Arc hosted Windows Server 2012 and 2012 R2 VM guests where administrators manually installed intermediate certs with expiration date of June 27, 2024.
rootCause: Manually installed intermediate certs have an expiration date of June 26, 2024. After expiration, year 4 ESU cannot be installed.
solution: Open connectivity to https://www.microsoft.com/pkiops/certs endpoint to allow Azure Arc to download critical intermediate certificates.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#185920

symptom: While installing Azure monitoring agent on Azure ARC enabled Kubernetes cluster with worker nodes running Oracle Linux 9.2, error: cannot initialize iptables table nat: Table does not exist (do you need to insmod?). The addon-token-adapter container encounters error modifying iptable rules.
rootCause: The infrastructure running on Oracle Linux 9.2 did not auto-load the iptable_nat module required for the MSI adapter to alter the nat tables on the node.
solution: Manually load the iptable_nat module on every node and restart: sudo modprobe iptable_nat. Verify with: lsmod | grep iptable_nat.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#186299

symptom: PowerShell script designed to enable ARC for virtual machines (VMs) in a vCenter in batch. Useful for large-scale operations managing hundreds or thousands of VMs.
solution: Use the PowerShell script from https://github.com/Azure/azure-arc-for-vmware-scripts/tree/main/batch-enablement which includes a README.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#186971

symptom: Onboarding script to connect VMware vCenter server to Azure Arc stuck at Step 2/5: Creating the Arc resource bridge and was getting terminated.
rootCause: The arc appliance extension was not installed because the download from PyPI was failing. The arc appliance show with redirect was stuck waiting for a prompt.
solution: Trust PyPI on the client machine so the arc appliance extension can be downloaded and installed.

symptom: Useful commands to work on Azure ARC enabled Kubernetes cluster Onboarding issues: kubectl get nodes, kubectl get pods, kubectl logs, etc.

symptom: GitOps Flux V2 extension installation failed with error Helm Installation failed on Azure ARC Red Hat OpenShift cluster.
rootCause: Customer did not add the security context constraint for OpenShift cluster as mentioned in the docs.
solution: Add security context constraints: oc adm policy add-scc-to-user nonroot system:serviceaccount:$NS:kustomize-controller and similar for helm-controller, notification-controller, source-controller.

symptom: Azure Arc ESU guidance for customers that manually installed intermediate certs with June 27, 2024 expiration dates. Year 4 ESU installation affected.
solution: Install or update the Azure Arc agent to version 1.4.0 or newer. Verify version with azcmagent version. Update intermediate certificates.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#191815

symptom: Deployment of a VM on vCenter via Azure Arc bridge using Bicep template fails with Resource type not found error, but manual deployment from ARC Machines in Azure portal succeeds.
rootCause: Incorrect vminstance resource syntax in Bicep template.
solution: Change vminstance resource: name from vmName to default, and replace dependsOn with scope: arcmachine.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#193003

symptom: Unable to onboard TKG (VMWare Tanzu Grid) Kubernetes cluster on Azure ARC due to Kyverno policy enforcement blocking required resource requests.
rootCause: Kyverno policy enforced in TKG cluster requires CPU and memory resource requests for all workloads. The admission webhook blocks the Azure ARC onboarding job.
solution: Temporarily scale down Kyverno admission controller: kubectl scale deploy kyverno-admission-controller -n kyverno-system --replicas=0. Proceed with onboarding, then scale back up.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#193954

symptom: Unable to delete stale Azure Arc resources via Azure portal or CLI. Resources show Expired status due to inactive Kubernetes clusters. Portal deletion times out.
rootCause: Inactive Kubernetes clusters cause Expired status. Portal cannot handle deletion of stale resources.
solution: Use Azure PowerShell: Remove-AzResource with -Force flag using the ARM ID of the cluster.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#194042

symptom: vCenter showing as Disconnected within Azure connected to Azure Arc Resource Bridge.
rootCause: Outdated VMware credentials used by Arc resource bridge.
solution: Update VMware credentials used by Arc resource bridge.

symptom: Azure Arc enable VMWare onboarding fails with error: tls failed to verify certificate x509 certificate signed by unknown authority. GuestInternetConnectivityError.
rootCause: Devices do not trust the certificate received during communication with Microsoft sites. Often caused by transparent proxies replacing original certificates.
solution: For transparent proxy: identify the intermediate device, export its CA certificate, and import it into the trust store on the workstation/appliance VM.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#196741

symptom: Unable to Onboard vCenter to Azure Arc.
solution: Running the onboarding script by using the FQDN of the vCenter instead of the IP address fixed the issue.

symptom: While Executing Script to Connect vCenter to Azure, failed in step 3/5 "Installing Cluster Extension" with error "Script Execution failed with Error: Provisioning state of cluster Extension is not Succeeded. Current state failed" Checking the Debug log you could find the error below DEBUG:cli.azure.cli.core.sdk.policies:{"id":"/subscriptions//resourceGroups//providers/Microsoft.ResourceConnector/appliances/RB-Azure-Arc-VCtest/providers/Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration/extensions/azure-vmwareoperator","name":"azure-vmwareoperator","type":"Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration/extensions","properties":{"extensionType":"microsoft.vmware","autoUpgradeMinorVersion":true,"releaseTrain":"Stable","version":null,"currentVersion":"1.0.91","scope":{"cluster":{"releaseNamespace":"azure-vmwareoperator"}},"provisioningState":"Failed","configurationSettings":{"Microsoft.CustomLocation.ServiceAccount":"azure-vmwareoperator"},"configurationProtectedSettings":{},"statuses":[{"code":"InstallationFailed","displayStatus":null,"level":null,"message":"Unable to download the Helm chart from the repourl https://azureprivatecloud.azurecr.io/helm/vmware/uksouth/stable/azure-vmwareoperator:1.0.91: Recommendation Please ensure that the helm repo is network reachable from the cluster : InnerError [failed to do request: Head \"https://azureprivatecloud.azurecr.io/v2/helm/vmware/uksouth/stable/azure-vmwareoperator/manifests/1.0.91\": tls: failed to verify certificate: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority], For general troubleshooting visit: https://aka.ms/k8s-extensions-TSG","time":null}],"aksAssignedIdentity":null,"isSystemExtension":false},"systemData":{"createdBy":null,"createdByType":null,"createdAt":"2024-10-07T14:48:20.2409209+00:00","lastModifiedBy":null,"lastModifiedByType":null,"lastModifiedAt":"2024-10-07T14:48:20.2409209+00:00"},"identity":{"type":"SystemAssigned","principalId":"06baab0f-785d-4679-8d6a-252408461ea5"}}
rootCause: VMware Cluster Extension URL "azureprivatecloud.azurecr.io" is not allowed over required port 443 as recommended
solution: Allow / Whitelist the VMware Cluster Extension URL "azureprivatecloud.azurecr.io" over the Port 443 as recommended by Azure Arc network requirements - Azure Arc | Microsoft LearnOnce allowed you could rerun the script again, it will skip appliance VM creating and will directly go to cluster extension step.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#196934

symptom: Arc Appliance run command failed during Appliance deployment with an error "http2: server sent GOAWAY and closed the connection", please run the command again with --force to cleanup environment.Arc Appliance run command failed during Appliance deployment with an error, please run the command again with --force to cleanup environment.{"errorCode": "SDKConfigAddAzureDataError","errorResponse": "{\n\"message\": \"Post \\\"https://uksouth.dp.kubernetesconfiguration.azure.com/azure-arc-appliance-k8sagents/GetLatestHelmPackagePath?api-version=2019-11-01-preview\\u0026releaseTrain=stable\\\": http2: server sent GOAWAY and closed the connection; LastStreamID=1, ErrCode=NO_ERROR, debug=\\\"\\\"\"\n}","errorMetadata": {"errorCategory": "SDKConfigValidationError"}}Script execution failed with error: Appliance VM creation failed.
rootCause: That happen due to some network call failing: C:\Windows\System32>curl -X POST "https://uksouth.dp.kubernetesconfiguration.azure.com/azure-arc-appliance-k8sagents/GetLatestHelmPackagePath?api-version=2019-11-01-preview&releaseTrain=stable" {"repositoryPath":"mcr.microsoft.com/azurearck8s/batch1/stable/appliance/azure-arc-k8sagents:0.30.10-appliance"} To Verify that : we can validate if http 2.0 connectivity to the endpoint is working. by running this command from windows PowerShell 7 (-HTTP Version flag not available in PowerShell 5). If http 1.1 works but not http 2.0, then some configuration in firewall is needed (like disabling SSL inspection). in order to identify which is blocked, you need to follow the procedure below for testing: Download and install PowerShell 7.xx Installing PowerShell on Windows - PowerShell | Microsoft Learn Run the elevated PS script below over PowerShell 7.xx and communicate the results back Invoke-WebRequest -Method Post -Verbose -UseBasicParsing -Uri "https://uksouth.dp.kubernetesconfiguration.azure.com/azure-arc-appliance-k8sagents/GetLatestHelmPackagePath?api-version=2019-11-01-preview&releaseTrain=stable" -HttpVersion 2.0 Invoke-WebRequest -Method Post -Verbose -UseBasicParsing -Uri "https://uksouth.dp.kubernetesconfiguration.azure.com/azure-arc-appliance-k8sagents/GetLatestHelmPackagePath?api-version=2019-11-01-preview&releaseTrain=stable" -HttpVersion 1.1 Expected success Case results Real failure Case results Conclusion: That mean HTTP 2.0 traffic is getting affected due to this firewall/ proxy blockage, CX should allow HTTP 2.0 over the Firewall / proxy so that get successfully reached.
solution: Enabling HTTP 2.0 through the firewall/proxy resolved the connectivity issue. Testing was completed successfully, and the script is now running as expected.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#196937

symptom: Azure Connected Machine Agent updates published to WSUS/CM are not applicable on Windows Server 2012 computers.
rootCause: The update metadata specified a minimum operating system version of Windows 2012 R2, which is version 6.3.9600. Windows Server 2012 is 6.2.9200, and so is not applicable.
solution: This is an error in the published metadata because Windows Server 2012 is still supported with the Azure Connected Machine Agent. The update detection logic will be corrected with the Azure Connected Machine Agent released in the 3rd or 4th week of November 2024.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#196976

symptom: The customer encountered connectivity and permission issues with an Azure ARC-enabled Kubernetes cluster, particularly while attempting to access the Workload tab within the Azure portal. Although the Kubernetes cluster was successfully onboarded to Azure ARC, with all pods in the `azure-arc` namespace appearing healthy and a stable connection achieved using the `az connectedk8s proxy` command, issues arose when interacting with specific portal features. When the customer attempted to access the Workload tab and entered the service account bearer token, the Azure portal threw an error. Investigation revealed that, instead of utilizing the provided token, the portal attempted to access resources with the customer’s user ID, which lacks the necessary permissions on the Kubernetes cluster. This caused consistent access errors, affecting the customer’s ability to view workloads as intended. Workload Tab Access Denied: Unable to open the Workload tab on the Azure portal for the ARC-enabled Kubernetes cluster after entering the service account bearer token. Error Message Displayed: After adding the bearer token, an error appears, indicating insufficient permissions or access issues, as the portal attempts to use the customer’s user ID instead of the token. 403 Error: Persistent 403 error occurs when trying to access the Workload tab, indicating a permissions issue on the portal side."Forbidden (403) deployments.apps is forbidden: User '[****]' cannot list resource 'deployments' in API group 'apps' at the cluster scope." Transient 599 Error: A temporary 599 error appears, often due to proxy settings or network configurations, which may intermittently block the URL."Error (599) Unable to reach the API server or API server is too busy to respond. {"message":"Failed to fetch","stack":"TypeError: Failed to fetch\n at https://portal.azure.com/Content/Dynamic/UvHUDxXWE-oN/js:113:24067","isError":true}" Similar Issues Across Clusters: The customer and their colleagues experience the same issue with multiple clusters, indicating a widespread problem.
rootCause: Portal Not Using the Right Token: When the customer tries to open the Workload tab, the Azure portal isn’t using the correct access token. Instead, it uses the customer’s own account, which doesn’t have permission to view the information on the cluster. This causes an error. Bug in Azure Portal: There’s a known issue on Azure’s end where even a valid access token isn’t enough to open the Workload tab, and it consistently shows an access denied error. Proxy Settings: The customer’s network setup, especially proxy settings, might occasionally block the connection to Azure ARC, leading to the temporary 599 error. Browser Issues: Sometimes, settings or add-ons in the customer’s web browser can interfere with connecting to the cluster, which might cause issues accessing certain parts of the portal.
solution: The 599 error was transient and it is due to customer's proxy settings bocking the url. Below are some troubleshooting steps for the 599 error. Customer's firewall/antivirussettings do not impact the endpoints specified in Quickstart: Connect an existing Kubernetes cluster to Azure Arc - Azure Arc | Microsoft Learn Customer's proxy server, if they are behind one, doesn't alter network requests to endpoints mentioned above. Try accessing the API server URI from a freshly installed browser. This is in case the existing customer browser might have extensions installed on it that could potentially interfere with any requests. Troubleshoot common Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes issues - Azure Arc | Microsoft Learn The 403 error is a consistent repro, it is a bug on portal end. Fixed the issue and pushed to a private flight to unblock customer. Home - Microsoft Azure
sourceRef: ContentIdea#197360

symptom: OpenSSL component (1.1.1) which part of akv secret provider extension, is no longer being supported and is recognized as a security vulnerability by vulnerability scanner.
rootCause: AKV secret provider extension version 1.5.5 has monitor pod (akvsecretsprovider-arc-monitoring-xxxxxxxxxx-xxxxx) which has Images with Open SSL component.
solution: Upgrade to next version of 1.5.6 where monitoring pod was removed in the version 1.5.6 az k8s-extension update --cluster-name --resource-group --cluster-type connectedClusters --name akvsecretsprovider --auto-upgrade false Use Azure Key Vault Secrets Provider extension to fetch secrets into Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes clusters - Azure Arc | Microsoft Learn
sourceRef: ContentIdea#197585

symptom:
Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes clusters should
have the Azure Policy extension installed
solution:
The reported error indicates that the Azure
Policy extension could not be installed on the Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes
cluster due to a communication issue between the extension agent on the cluster
and Azure services. You need to ensure that the Kubernetes cluster can reach
Azure services. Specifically, verify that the cluster can access the following
endpoints: 
 
  https://management.azure.com  
  https://agent.kubernetesconfiguration.azure.com
       
  https://policy.kubernetesconfiguration.azure.com 
  also ensure no firewalls or network security
groups are blocking outbound traffic on required ports (typically 443 for
HTTPS).
sourceRef: ContentIdea#199286

symptom:
When attempting to
onboard an OpenShift cluster to Azure Arc, the kube-aad proxy deployment and the pod replica's does not run.  Running the deployment command results in the
following error message:  kubectl get deployment "deployment name" -o yamlError:message: 'pods "kube-aad-proxy-66f47dcdb-" is forbidden: unable to validate against
      any security context constraint: [provider "anyuid": Forbidden: not usable by
      user or serviceaccount, spec.volumes[1]: Invalid value: "hostPath": hostPath
      volumes are not allowed to be used, spec.volumes[2]: Invalid value: "hostPath":
      hostPath volumes are not allowed to be used, provider restricted-v2: .initContainers[0].runAsUser:
      Invalid value: 1001: must be in the ranges: [1000700000, 1000709999], provider
      restricted-v2: .containers[1].privileged: Invalid value: true: Privileged containers
      are not allowed, provider restricted: .initContainers[0].runAsUser: Invalid
      value: 1001: must be in the ranges: [1000700000, 1000709999], provider restricted:
      .containers[1].privileged: Invalid value: true: Privileged containers are not
      allowed, provider "nonroot-v2": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount,
      provider "nonroot": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount, provider
      "hostmount-anyuid": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount, provider
      "elasticsearch-scc": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount, provider
      "machine-api-termination-handler": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount,
      provider "hostnetwork-v2": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount,
      provider "hostnetwork": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount, provider      "hostaccess": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount, spec.volumes[6]:
      Invalid value: "csi": csi volumes are not allowed to be used, spec.volumes[7]:
      Invalid value: "emptyDir": emptyDir volumes are not allowed to be used, spec.volumes[9]:
      Invalid value: "emptyDir": emptyDir volumes are not allowed to be used, provider
      "netobserv-ebpf-agent": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount, provider      "infraviz-privileged-scc": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount,
      provider "node-exporter": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount, provider
      "appdynamics-infraviz": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount, provider
      "privileged": Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount, provider "privileged-genevalogging":
      Forbidden: not usable by user or serviceaccount]'
    reason: FailedCreate
rootCause:
The error is caused by
missing SCC permissions
solution:
To resolve this issue,
grant the necessary permissions and restart the kube-aad proxy pod by following
these steps: 
 Switch to the azure-arc project
     in OpenShift:oc project azure-arc
 Add the privileged SCC
     to the service account used by the kube-aad proxy:oc adm policy
add-scc-to-user privileged
system:serviceaccount:azure-arc:azure-arc-kube-aad-proxy-saAfter executing these
steps, restart the kube-aad proxy pod to apply the changes.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#199403

symptom:
Arc resource bridge
deployment fails using a deployment script. The script
errors out and ends after vSphere credentials are entered with error message: "Code: ResourceNotFound Message: The Resource
'Microsoft.ResourceConnector/appliances/arc-resourcebridge-' under resource
group 'ResourceGroup' was not found."
rootCause:
Azure CLI v2.70.0 released a
breaking change which triggers this error in arcappliance CLI extension v1.4.0
and below.
solution:
When
     trying to deploy the Arc Resource Bridge using a template, the deployment
     fails with an error: "vSphere credentials are entered with
     error message: Code: ResourceNotFound Message: The Resource
     'Microsoft.ResourceConnector/appliances/arc-resourcebridge' under resource
     group ‘ResourceBridge' was not found" 
 Verify
     and enter the vCenter FQDN or IP Address in the format
     FQDN:PORT or IP:PORT 
 There may be an issue with the vCenter password entered by the
     client. Verify if the credentials entered meet the password requirements. 
 Check the version of az-cli used by the client using Az –version 
 If the client is using Azure CLI version 2.70.0, this is a kown
     issue where there may be issues encountered with the arcappliance. 
 A fix is in development but in the meantime, we need to ask the
     client to downgrade Azure CLI to v2.69.0. 
 If the client
     used the Azure CLI installer, you can uninstall the current version and
     install Azure CLI v2.69.0 from the Azure CLI installation page.  
 If the client
     used the pip installer, you can run the following command to downgrade: pip install azure-cli==2.69.0. 
 Also, for the
     Arc-enabled VMware onboarding script, you need to comment out the below
     code in the script to not update the AZ CLI to latest again:   if (shouldInstallAzCli) {    installAzCli64Bit }  
 This change
     should resolve the issue, and the deployment should now be successful.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#200360

rootCause: The problem is that Vsphere FQDN was not resolvable by the dnsservers  "Reconciler error" err="unexpected error while probing vcenter for infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1, Kind=VSphereCluster default/xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx: failed to create vCenter session: failed to create client: Post \"https://vspherefqdn:443/sdk\": dial tcp: lookup vspherefqdn.local on IPadress: no such host" controller="vsphereCluster" controllerGroup="infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io" controllerKind="VSphereCluster" VSphereCluster="default/xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx" namespace="default" name="xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx" reconcileID="xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx" Customer needs ensure the vsphere endpoint is reachable from within the ARB VM.
solution: The client added an A record in the DNS for the vSphere host to be reachable.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#201055

symptom:
Onboarding
of Rancher Kubernetes Engine cluster to Azure Arc is failing with below error.  WARNING:
Downloading helm client for first time. This can take few minutes...
ERROR: The command failed with an unexpected error. Here is the traceback:
ERROR: [Errno 2] No such file or directory:
'/root/.azure/kubectl-client/kubectl'
rootCause:
storage.googleapis.com
is used to download kubectl release in case the kubectl is not present in the
customer environment. It
is observed that the endpoint storage.googleapis.com is being blocked
resulting the onboarding operation is getting failed.  It
is needed for a successful onboarding of Rancher Kubernetes Engine cluster
to Azure Arc but the endpoint didn't listed in below article. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-arc/kubernetes/network-requirements?tabs=azure-cloud#details
solution:
From
Azure CLI version 2.68.0 onwards, CLI is downloading the binary
from dl.k8s.io/release for
all cloud envs except mooncake, where the binary is downloaded from mirror.azure.cn. Hence,
we no need to allow endpoint storage.googleapis.com for
onboarding Rancher Kubernetes Engine cluster to Azure Arc.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#201260

symptom: Can I safely rename the Azure Arc Resource Bridge virtual appliance in VMware vCenter after deployment?
rootCause: The name of the appliance is tied to the underlying Azure resource registration, and renaming it in vCenter may break communication between Azure and your on-premises environment.
solution: No. Renaming the Resource Bridge appliance in vCenter is not supported
sourceRef: ContentIdea#201276

symptom:
The
Arc resource bridge is offline.
rootCause:
The
ARB pods were not scheduled because of taints.
solution:
#SSH into the
controlplane VM IP  ssh clouduser@127.0.0.1 -i .\logkey - Check if the pods
are offline in Arc resource bridge. By running the below command we can check
if the pods are offline or not. Kubectl get pods –all-namespaces - If it is offline
then check why the Pods are offline. kubectl describe <Pod name>-n <namespace of
the pod> - If we get the
output that is because of taints the pods were not scheduled. - Run the below
command to remove the taint. kubectl taint nodes xxxxxxx-   xxxxx is the taint
which we will get from the kubectl describe node command.   Check if all the
Pods are running.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#201811

symptom:
Unable
to onboard the VMware VMs to the Azure portal.
rootCause:
After redeploying
VMs in vcenter and when we try to enable guest management in vcenter it will
fail with error that VMs are already part of the resource group. If we check further
we see that there is already a VM present with same name in the resource group
and the VM status is guest management disconnected.
solution: Delete the ARM resource and reenable, It only deletes the VM from Azure and will not delete the VM in Vcenter. Then onboard the VMs.   az connectedvmware vm delete --debug --yes --subscription <>--resource-group <> --name <VM-name>
sourceRef: ContentIdea#201819

symptom:
Existing
ARC VMware Resource Bridge appears offline in Azure Portal; however, it appears
as running in vCenter and rebooted the VM.
rootCause:
Due
to some infra issue on cx environment or credential change.
solution:
1-Reboot
VM on Vmware 
2-update the credentials using the following Command:   az connectedvmware vcenter connect --custom-location <name
of the custom location> --location <Azure region>  --name
<name of the vCenter resource in
Azure>       --resource-group <resource
group for the vCenter resource>  --username   <username
for the vSphere account>  --password  <password to the vSphere
account> 3-Customer can follow the Disaster Recovery steps to recreate the resource bridge: 
 Take a note of the names for
     resource bridge, custom location and VCenter on azure portal. 
 Delete the resource bridge VM
     (if not already) from the vCenter (name follows pattern *-control-plane-*
     ) 
 Delete the resource bridge
     from azure 
 Download the onboarding
     script from azure after providing some dummy values for the name of
     resource bridge, custom location and vcenter  
 Replace the dummy values with
     the current name of resource bridge, custom location and vcenter 
 Run the onboarding script  Perform
disaster recovery operations - Azure Arc | Microsoft Learn    We
shouldn't remove VCenter, above flow only delete and recreate ARB. We
can think of above flow as replacing non-working ARB with new working ARB
without touching any other resources.
